turns.content: do you think a cs degree is still worth it in 2025? one of our junior devs is asking me for advice and i honestly don't know what to tell him

It depends heavily on what they want to do. The honest answer is more nuanced than "yes" or "no":

**Still worth it if:**
- They want to work on systems-level stuff (distributed systems, databases, compilers, infrastructure)
- They're interested in research or highly technical roles where theory matters
- They want to work outside their home country — a degree makes visa processes much easier
- They want a structured learning environment and the discipline it provides

**Less necessary if:**
- They want to do web/app development — bootcamps and self-teaching can get them there faster
- They're already working as a dev (like your junior) and building real experience
- They're mainly motivated by getting a job quickly

**The uncomfortable truth:**
A degree teaches you how to think about problems, not how to write production code. Those are different skills. But the thinking part is what separates someone who can architect a system from someone who can only follow tutorials.

What's the junior's situation? Are they self-taught and considering going back to school, or deciding whether to start one?

he's 22, self-taught, been with us for about 8 months. solid on the practical side, ships features, writes decent code. but i can tell he has gaps when it comes to the fundamentals — like he'll build something that works but doesn't understand why certain approaches scale and others don't. he asked me if he should go do a degree part-time

That's a very specific and useful gap to identify. "Works but doesn't scale" is exactly the kind of problem that theoretical CS training addresses — algorithms, complexity theory, data structures beyond the basics, understanding distributed system tradeoffs.

For his specific situation, a few options:

**Full CS degree part-time:**
- Pros: Fills the theoretical gaps systematically, gets the credential, opens doors internationally
- Cons: 4-5 years part-time, expensive depending on where, massive time commitment while working full-time

**Targeted learning (no degree):**
- Work through a rigorous algorithms course (MIT OCW 6.006, Stanford's Algorithms Specialization on Coursera)
- Read "Designing Data-Intensive Applications" by Kleppmann — this one book would fill most of his scaling intuition gaps
- Study system design specifically: how do you reason about throughput, latency, consistency tradeoffs?

**Hybrid:**
- Some universities offer certificate programs or individual modules you can take without enrolling in a full degree. Gets some of the rigor without the 4-year commitment.

What's your own background? Did you go the degree route or self-taught? That context might help you give him more grounded advice.
i did my bsc in informatik at tu munich. graduated 2018. so yeah i went the degree route and i'm biased but i also know exactly how much of that education i actually use day to day

TU Munich Informatik is no joke — that's one of the strongest CS programs in Germany. So you've got the full picture: rigorous theory, the good and the painful.

How much of it do you actually use? I'd guess it breaks down something like:

- **Directly use daily**: Maybe 15-20% — data structures, basic algorithms, some systems concepts
- **Use indirectly (shapes how you think)**: Another 30-40% — complexity analysis, concurrency models, formal reasoning about correctness
- **Never touched again**: The rest — whatever theoretical deep dives didn't connect to your career path

But that 30-40% "shapes how you think" category is the invisible value. It's why you can look at a scaling problem and reason about it from first principles instead of Googling "how to make my API faster."

Does that match your experience?

pretty much exactly. the theoretical cs at tu munich was genuinely brutal — i had a discrete math professor who seemed personally offended by incorrect proofs — but that's honestly the reason i can reason about distributed systems properly now instead of just cargo-culting microservices like half the berlin tech scene

"Personally offended by incorrect proofs" — every good math professor has that energy. But you're making the exact point: the pain of rigorous theory translates directly into engineering judgment. When you're designing a payment pipeline, you're implicitly using concepts from distributed systems theory, consistency models, failure mode analysis — stuff that's hard to pick up from blog posts.

The cargo-culting microservices thing is real. Berlin has a lot of "we use Kubernetes because Netflix uses Kubernetes" engineering, where people adopt patterns without understanding the problems those patterns solve.

For your junior, here's maybe the most honest framing you can give him:

*"I went to TU Munich, and the theory was brutal but it's the foundation for everything I do now. But I was also 18 and could commit full-time. You're 22, already working, already shipping code. Your path is different. The question isn't 'should I get a degree' — it's 'what specific gaps do I need to fill and what's the most efficient way to fill them?'"*

If his gap is scaling intuition specifically, the Kleppmann book + a solid algorithms course might give him 80% of what he needs without the 4-year commitment. But if he wants the credential and the full foundation, a part-time program at a solid German university is very affordable compared to most countries.

that's a good way to frame it. he's german too so tuition is basically free which removes the biggest argument against it. i think what i'll tell him is: read kleppmann first, do one of those algorithms courses, and if after that he still feels the gaps then consider the degree. no need to go nuclear on day one

That's solid advice — incremental approach. Kleppmann + algorithms is a 3-6 month investment. If that fills the gaps, great, he saved 4 years. If it doesn't, he'll at least start the degree knowing what he wants to get out of it, which makes the whole experience more useful.

And you're right that free tuition changes the calculus completely. In the US this conversation would be "is a $120k degree worth it" — in Germany it's "is 4 years of your time worth it" which is a much cleaner question.

One more thing worth mentioning to him: if he does go the degree route, TU Berlin, HU Berlin, or FU Berlin all have decent Informatik programs and he wouldn't have to relocate. Part-time while keeping his job is very doable at a Berlin university.
yeah i was going to suggest tu berlin specifically. although part of me wants to tell him to go to munich just for the suffering. character building. the mensas are better too

Sending juniors to Munich for "character building" is a power move. Though the real character building at TU Munich is surviving the Garching campus commute, not the coursework.

But realistically, TU Berlin is the right call for him — stays employed, stays in his network, gets the fundamentals without uprooting his life. And Berlin Informatik is solid, it's just not the masochistic gauntlet that Munich is.

The Mensa point is valid though. Munich university food is unreasonably good for institutional cafeteria food.

lol the garching commute, you're not wrong. alright i think i have a good framework for the conversation with him now. start with kleppmann and algorithms, evaluate after, tu berlin if he wants the full thing, and a gentle suggestion that he's not as far behind as he thinks he is. because honestly he's doing fine for 8 months in

That last point matters most. Self-taught devs at 8 months tend to be hyper-aware of what they don't know, which is actually a good sign — but it can spiral into imposter syndrome if nobody tells them they're on track.

Your framework is solid:
1. Kleppmann + algorithms course (targeted gap-filling)
2. Evaluate after 3-6 months (does he still feel the gaps?)
3. TU Berlin part-time if yes (affordable, no relocation)
4. Reassurance that 8 months in and shipping features is genuinely good

He's lucky to have a senior who went through the rigorous degree path and can give him an honest assessment instead of the generic "you don't need a degree" tech Twitter take. Good mentoring.
appreciate that. alright gonna go talk to him before feierabend. happy new year by the way, first work week of 2025 and i'm already doing career counseling instead of actual coding. great start

Career counseling IS actual work — arguably higher leverage than any code you'd write today. One good conversation can redirect someone's entire trajectory.

Happy new year. May 2025 bring clean deployments and zero legacy payroll migrations. Go catch him before he leaves.
